news_communities.title: Saugus Gardens in the Summer
news_communities.author: Jim Mitchell
news_communities.summary:
Here’s what’s blooming in town this week to make your walks more enjoyable

 

By Laura Eisener

 

The full moon this month will occur on Wednesday, July 29. July’s moon is often called the buck moon, because this is the time of year that deer develop their antlers.

The photo above shows flowers and foliage of a silk tree, sometimes called mimosa (Albizia julibrisson ‘E.H. Wilson’), in the backyard of Arlene and Gene Decareau. Despite its not being close to the sidewalk, the tree’s height makes it visible over the roof of the house, especially in the afternoon when the sun shines on it. There are several examples of this species in Saugus, but I would still consider it a somewhat unusual tree.

The foliage comes out fairly late in the spring, but when the compound leaves unfold, they are very graceful and lacy looking. At night or on a very dark cloudy day, the leaflets droop to save moisture, and the first time someone notices this they might be likely to think the tree is sick, but soon after the sun returns the leaves go back to normal. There is no fall color; the leaves seem to drop abruptly at the first frost. The often-used name mimosa actually belongs to some other tropical plants with similar foliage, so it can be confusing, especially in warm parts of the world where 400 species currently identified as mimosa grow.

The showy part of the flowers on a silk tree is not the petals, but the silky, threadlike stamens, which are extended beyond the rest of the flower parts in each inflorescence. You might not expect this to be a member of the legume family (Fabaceae) by looking at the flowers, because the blossoms do not resemble the usual “pea” shaped blossom that you would find on familiar family members like common garden peas (Pisum sativum) and garden beans (Phaseolus spp.) or other hardy trees that are members of the family, such as honey locust (Gleditsia triacanthos) or Japanese pagoda tree (Styphnolobium japonicum). Once the flowers are gone, however, the seed pods are fairly similar to other members of the family. The small flat light green pods of the silk tree, through which you can see the outlines of the seeds, look very similar to snow pea pods although tougher and not considered edible. Parts of the tree have been used medicinally, but generally we think of it as primarily ornamental.

Native to a wide area from the Near East to China, most places it grows have milder climates than ours. Less than a decade after the Declaration of Independence was signed, the French botanist Andre Michaux brought some silk trees to his new home in South Carolina, and they have spread throughout much of the southern U.S. and South America, but for over a century none were found to be hardy enough for the northern part of the U.S.

In 1918, the famous plant collector from the Arnold Arboretum, Ernest Wilson, discovered a tree in Korea with deeper pink flower color than usual, as well as growing in a location that had colder winters than this tree usually tolerated. He brought seeds back to Harvard University’s Arnold Arboretum in Jamaica Plain. One of the original seedlings, now a substantial tree, still grows in what is known as the Explorers Garden on the south slope of Bussey Hill in the Arboreum. Now over 100 years old, this hardy specimen is multi-stemmed and spreading in shape, and attracts many visitors with its showy, long blooming blossoms. This variety — now officially called the cultivar ‘E.H. Wilson’ (Albizia julibrisson ‘E.H. Wilson’) but originally called ‘rosea’ — tolerates a colder climate than most members of this species.

The trees I have seen in Saugus seem to be generally of this hardy variety. The species has been planted in the South, where it has proven to be somewhat invasive but also susceptible to a wilt disease that so far does not usually survive New England winters. In our climate it has not proved to be invasive, nor has it been especially disease-prone, but these tendencies in gardens farther south have led to some reluctance on the part of nurseries to make this tree widely available. However, a healthy silk tree is a delight in bloom for much of the summer and gives a somewhat tropical feel to northern gardens at the height of the season. Happily, there are one or two gardens in most Saugus neighborhoods where these trees are blooming for passersby to enjoy.

news_communities.title: Irene’s Beautiful Day for a Car Show
news_communities.author: Jim Mitchell
news_communities.summary:
Mom’s Cancer Fighting Angels features 230 cars, motorcycles and trucks at 12th annual event

 

By Joanie Allbee

 

Great weather greeted the visitors who gathered in the parking lot of the Saugus Middle-High School on Sunday (July 19) for the 12th annual Mom’s Cancer Fighting Angels Car Show Cruise Night.

Guy Moley, the chief founder and organizer of the car show, respectfully credits the beautiful blue skies day to his mother Irene Comeau, who sadly passed away over a decade ago with esophageal cancer. “She is watching out for us up there, she sees what we are trying to do and gave us great weather to raise money to fight cancer,” Guy said.

Irene is the whole reason Guy and his wife Brenda, niece Alexis, sister Jodi Lyn and friends John Melanson and John Gilmore started the Mom’s Cancer Fighting Angels Car Show. Guy also credits Bill Pappas for his mentorship.

Guests gathered around their favorite vehicles with ballot sheets in hand, judging their best choices out of 230 cars, motorcycles and trucks. Brenda Moley multitasked between raffle prize tickets, 50/50 and providing information. Batman was in true character form and busy posing with families for photos. Disc Jockey Little Jimmy entertained with his stack of records. Boston Ice Cream was waiting on lines of customers looking for cool treats on a hot day. Hearty lunches were served from the J&M Italian eatery food truck. Raising Cane’s provided a games and prizes booth for the kids. The owners of cars, trucks and motorcycles were all eager to participate and came from all over to help raise money to fight cancer and have good eats and good times. Fox Run Dance Company put on several impromptu dance shows to much applause.

Saugus letter carrier of 25 years Suzanne Titus, owner of her 2023 Harley-Davidson Tri Glide, expressed her feelings of gratitude about the event. “Thanks to Guy Moley for raising awareness and money for fighting cancer,” she said.

Todd Angilly sang our American National Anthem and added a Canadian National Anthem in honor of a fellow Canadian fighting the battle with cancer. Todd was proudly wearing his Legion Riders Chapter 101 motorcycle jacket with all his emblem patches.

Sgt. Ned Shenick, a third-generation Scottish bagpipes player, filled the air with solemn sounds as he faded into the distance, honoring those who lost their lives to cancer. Alexis Comeau rang out the bells for those who recently passed away – like Charlie Zapolski, who died March 5th.

Guy Moley and Alexis Comeau gave out the trophies to elated participants. Jim Reece of Little Jimmy Stack of Records was awarded a plaque of honor for making a difference at the car shows by spinning tunes and faithfulness to Mom’s Cancer Fighting Angels.

A few of the Car Show trophies were:

  • The Charlie Zapoloski Trophy. This trophy had a bottle of Charlie’s favorite attached, which was a bottle of Carlo Rossi Burgundy. Ron Wallace was the recipient. Charlie used to love to take photos of it.
  • Best In Show Trophy went to Don Lee and wife Josie, who won with their Shelby Mustang.
  • Cancer Survivor’s Choice Trophy went to Rick Bicknell for his 1987 Monte Carlo.
  • Lucky raffle winners were chosen and went home with their gifts.

Smiling faces were everywhere to be seen at Mom’s Cancer Fighting Angels car show. The Fundraiser was a roaring success. Money was tallied at the end of the event to the total of $3,633 (however, some donations are still trickling in). All the money will go to the American Cancer Society. Saugus and surrounding area visitors and vehicle participants all had the same thing to say about how this was a great event, and they all gave their thanks to Mom’s Cancer Fighting Angels for their selfless long hours of hard work and providing local family fun and a place to show their vehicles for a worthy cause.

news_communities.title: The Sounds of Saugus
news_communities.author: Jim Mitchell
news_communities.summary:
By Mark E. Vogler

 

Good morning, Saugus

With the Board of Selectmen signing the State Primary Election Warrant last week, Town Clerk Ellen Schena and her staff have about five weeks to complete preparation for the upcoming Sept. 1 primary election. A major order of business is signing up enough election workers – both paid and volunteers.

Every two years, there is a state election which involves a September primary. With school not being in session yet, it becomes more challenging for the Town Clerk to recruit civic-minded Saugus High School juniors and seniors. So a flyer began circulating this week headlined “CALLING ALL SAUGUS HIGH JUNIORS AND SENIORS,” with the hopes of attracting civic-minded teens who want to help the elections run smoothly this fall: the State Primary (Sept. 1) and the Nov. 3 State General Election.

“Come work for Community Services to fulfill your high school hours OR you can be a Paid $$$$$$ Election Worker,” the flyer stated.

For those Saugus juniors and seniors who are interested in being paid or volunteer election workers, here are the particulars:

  • 16 years old can work part-time shifts (6 to 8 hours)
  • 17 & 18 year olds can work full shifts (8 to 12 hours)
  • The Town Clerk’s Office can accommodate any hours for interested students.

“Great to have on your College Applications/Resumes,” the flyer noted.

Those who are interested should stop by Town Hall or contact the Town Clerk’s Office to apply to work. Ask for Jacey Ruisi, Saugus Election Coordinator, at 781-231-4102 for more details.

A second flyer went out directed at adults who want to work the polls and earn some extra cash. The Town Clerk’s Office is looking to fill specific shifts: all day, or 6 a.m. to 2 p.m., 2 p.m. to 9 p.m. or swing shifts (accommodating hours). For those who are interested in applying for any of those paid positions, please contact the Town Clerk’s Office at 781-231-4102 or 4013.

 

Saugus United Parish Food Pantry

The Saugus United Parish Food Pantry will be open today (Friday, July 24), from 9:30-11 a.m. at 50 Essex St. in the basement of Cliftondale Congregational Church. The food pantry welcomes all neighbors facing food insecurity on Friday mornings. Volunteers are also welcome. Please call the Food Pantry Office (781-233-2663) or go to the website (cliftondalecc.org) for details.

 

This week’s “Shout Outs”

We received a “Shout Out” this week, from Coach Chris Tarantino, who runs the Saugus Sachems Track Camp: “This shout out is for all those involved in the successful Saugus Sachems Summer Track Camp, athletes coaches counselors parents and extended family. For their hard work, support and dedication which led to first place showing at the program CLCF Summer Showdown down in Cranston, Rhode Island. Eighteen teams competed and yes this is our first time finishing first at that meet, this now being our fifth year attending previously finishing second and third.”

 

Want to “Shout Out” a fellow Saugonian?

This is an opportunity for our paper’s readers to single out – in a brief mention – remarkable acts or achievements by Saugus residents or an act of kindness or a nice gesture. Just send an email (mv***@*****st.net) with a mention in the subject line of “An Extra Shout Out.” No more than a paragraph; anything longer might lend itself to a story and/or a photo.

 

Free Summer concerts series underway

You know it’s truly summer in Saugus when the Wednesday night crowds start to gather at the Saugus Iron Works National Historic Site, where they sing, clap and dance to the music. It’s that time of year again. Another summer of free outdoor concerts over an eight-week period at the Iron Works began on July 1.

This marks the fifth consecutive summer that the Saugus Public Library and the National Park Service are collaborating on a local outdoor event that seems to get more popular every year. The Saugus Cultural Council (a local arm of the Mass Cultural Council, a state agency) is also a key sponsor of the event. The concerts are free and open to the public. The concert series celebrates local musical talents in Essex County and creates opportunities for residents and visitors to engage with the park during evening hours.

Shows begin at 6 p.m. each Wednesday in July and August. People can bring blankets and chairs, snacks, etc. Don’t forget to bring a friend, too, to enjoy a couple of hours of music outdoors. The events take place on the stage behind the museum building, and spectators can settle down on the upper lawn. It is suitable for all ages. Restrooms are available until nearly 8 p.m. when the concerts conclude. If weather looks bad for any particular Wednesday, concertgoers are urged to check the library website (www.sauguspubliclibrary.org), which will update for cancellations on that day.

This year’s lineup features a combination of new bands and familiar faces. Concerts begin at 6 p.m. on the Big Lawn each Wednesday through August 19. This week’s headliner (July 15) was Renee & the Renegades, a group that brings a lively Country/Rock blend. Here is the rest of the schedule for the summer concert series:

  • July 29: Ditto Band (Acoustic American Rock).
  • August 5: The Implications (Rock & Country).
  • August 12: Squeezebox Stompers (Cajun, Zydeco, Blues, Folk).
  • August 19: Coco Brown & The Invitation (Motown & Soul).

Watch https://sauguspubliclibrary.org for weather updates and cancellations.

 

Starlight Cinema Tuesday night

Saugus Community Television invites residents of all ages to enjoy an evening of family fun under the stars at its annual Starlight Cinema Movie Night on Tuesday, July 28. This year’s featured film is the beloved family adventure “Jumanji” (1995), starring Robin Williams. The movie is about a magical board game that brings jungle dangers to life.

The event will be held at Parcher Field (located next to Kasabuski Rink behind Target). Family activities begin at 5:30 p.m., and the movie will start promptly at 7:30 p.m. The rain date will be one week later on August 4. This year’s event will feature a large LED movie screen, allowing the film to begin before sunset while still providing an outstanding viewing experience for everyone in attendance. The earlier starting time makes it easier for families with young children to enjoy the evening and return home at a reasonable hour.

Starlight Cinema is a free community event open to everyone. Complimentary popcorn will be provided, while supplies last, and guests are welcome to bring their own snacks and nonalcoholic beverages. A variety of food and dessert options from local food vendors will be available for purchase throughout the evening. Featured vendors: Tiffany’s Tasty Treats, offering gourmet brownies, cookies and gluten-free and sugar-free desserts; Bono Appetit, serving specialty sandwiches, including pulled pork grilled cheese and paninis; and Soc’s Ice Cream, featuring refreshing frozen treats for a summer evening.

Beginning at 5:30 p.m., families can enjoy a variety of free activities: interactive games displayed on the LED screen, a festive photo booth featuring a giant popcorn-themed arch, giant Jenga and other family-friendly entertainment. Local businesses and community organizations will also be on hand with informational displays, giveaways and activities.

Saugus Television will offer exclusive Starlight Cinema commemorative T-shirts for purchase. The shirts feature an original design created exclusively for the event by Saugus Television’s own John Prudent. Attendees will be able to select their preferred shirt size, and the shirts will be custom printed. Shirts will also be available during Saugus Founders Day for those who are unable to attend the movie night.

Starlight Cinema is made possible through the generous support of the Saugus Cultural Council, Richard A. Kowalsky Insurance Agency, Inc., community partners, local businesses and sponsors. Saugus Television is currently seeking additional sponsors to help support this free community event. Businesses and organizations interested in sponsorship opportunities are encouraged to contact Saugus Television for more information.

Volunteers are also needed to assist with event setup, activities and operations throughout the evening. Anyone interested in volunteering is encouraged to contact Maureen Whitcomb at Saugus Community Television.

Building on the anticipated success of this year’s event, Saugus Television hopes to continue the Starlight Cinema series with a special Halloween-themed movie night later this year.

“This is the first time Saugus is doing the movie,” said Whitcomb, the station’s administrative assistant.

“Saugus TV wants to do a second one for Halloween, then wants to continue them next summer too. Similar to Wakefield and surrounding towns,” she said.

Additional details will be announced as plans are finalized. Continued community support through sponsorships and volunteers will help make future free family events possible.

Residents are encouraged to bring lawn chairs, blankets and bug spray and to gather with family and friends for an evening of outdoor entertainment, community spirit and summertime fun.

For more information about Starlight Cinema, sponsorship opportunities or volunteer opportunities, please contact Saugus Community Television.

 

Cars and Cops Aug. 1

The Seventh Annual Cars and Cops Car Show is set for Saturday, Aug. 1, from 9 a.m. to 1 p.m. in the parking lot of the Saugus Middle-High School at 1 Pierce Memorial Drive. The rain date is Aug. 8. The Cars and Cops Car Show of Massachusetts is a widely known family fun car show that occurs every year in the summer, put on by Saugus Police Officer Domenic Montano and his amazing team that wants to give back to their community. The Cars and Cops Car Show of Massachusetts first started in September of 2018 and grew yearly. What originally started as a 100-125 vehicle car show grew to 300-plus cars in 2021.

 

KOC Crafts fair Aug. 8

The Saugus Knights of Columbus No. 1829 will host a crafts fair on Saturday, Aug. 8, from 9 a.m. to 3 p.m. at 57 Appleton St. in Saugus: baked goods, candles, handmade textiles, jewelry, wood carving and much more. Visitors can also enjoy making their own sundaes or enjoy popcorn, hot dogs, hot coffee, donuts and raffles.

 

Summer Reading time at the library

The 2026 Summer Reading Program at the Saugus Public Library is underway and will last through Friday, Aug. 21. Here’s a summary of the program:

WHO: Kids Pre-K through 6th grade.

WHAT: Kids read the books they love, log their reading and earn prizes! They’ll enjoy reading what they love, free programs, crafts and activities.

WHY: Don’t lose those hard-earned skills! Summer reading plays a vital role in helping reduce the “Summer Slide” – the learning loss experienced between school years – which can leave students significantly behind their peers. The Saugus Public Schools recommend kids read at least 20 minutes every day. We also recommend reading to your child, even as they become an independent reader. Talk about what you’ve read together. Help them find books they like – we can help with that. Make it a fun part of a daily routine they look forward to!

PRIZES: Every reader wins prizes, and books to keep. We’ll also raffle vouchers for ice cream, pizza, roller skating, mini golf, bowling and much more. Grand prize drawings include tickets to Canobie Lake Park and Nova Adventure Park!

HOW: Download the Beanstack App!

  • CREATE AN ACCOUNT: If this is your first time participating in one of the SPL’s reading challenges using Beanstack, here are step-by-step instructions [https://www.loom.com/share/0efe0cc7c0f64b96b3c0a38ade8b04f6] on how to create a Beanstack account. If you’ve used Beanstack before, simply register for our 2026 Summer Reading Challenge.
  • REGISTER FOR A CHALLENGE: This video [https://www.loom.com/share/9cfd52f03a4c4444bc9ab86d94b51cea] will show you how to register for a reading challenge using the app.
  • LOG YOUR READING: This video [https://www.loom.com/share/fda67bebf80e443faaad2529fa3ca486] will show you how to log your reading using the mobile app.
  • FROM A COMPUTER: If you prefer not to use the app, here’s a link to access the SPL’s 2026 Kids Summer Reading Challenge using a computer. Beanstack Link [https://sauguspubliclibrary.beanstack.com/reader365]
  • Or Register in Person: Stop by the library to register in person and use our paper reading log.
  • Download free e-books and e-audiobooks from the library!

Can’t make it to the library? Our digital collections provide easy access to the books kids love, 24 hours a day seven days a week. Download free, fresh titles all summer using the hoopla and Libby apps! [https://www.sauguspubliclibrary.org/children/family]

 

Veterans Day Bricks Program is Back

The Saugus Veterans Council is pleased to reinstate the Brick Program! After our vendor retired last year (thank you, Del from Pavlok, for working with us over the years), we have found another engraver who will be taking our orders for Veterans Day! Proceeds from the brick sales are devoted to the upkeep of Saugus Veterans Park. If you are interested in purchasing a brick, a 4×8″ is $100 (three lines) and an 8×8″ (five lines) is $200. Please contact Corinne Riley (781-231-7995) for an application or more information. All orders must be in no later than Sept. 10.

news_communities.title: ABCC Probes Liquor Law Violations
news_communities.author: Jim Mitchell
news_communities.summary:
Three and a half hours of drinking at Saugus bar proved fatal for wrong-way driver and the state trooper he killed

 

By Mark E. Vogler

 

Tribu Mexican Kitchen & Bar on Rte. 1 North in Saugus faces possible action against its liquor license after an investigation determined the restaurant was the final stop for an intoxicated wrong-way driver who collided head-on with a state trooper in a fatal crash that killed both men on May 6 in Lynnfield. Tribu served Hernan Ramon Marrero, 50, of Roslindale, nine alcoholic drinks during three-and-a-half hours shortly before the crash, according to a report released last week by Essex County District Attorney Paul F. Tucker.

“Toxicological testing showed Marrero’s Blood Alcohol Concentration (BAC) to be 0.192 g/100 mL, over twice the legal limit,” the report noted. “Also present in his blood were Bupropion (Wellbutrin) 30 ng/mL, an antidepressant and smoking inhibitor, and Hydroxybupropion 100 ng/mL, a metabolite of that drug.”

Thirty-year-old Trooper Kevin T. Trainor, of Georgetown, who died from injuries suffered in the crash, “demonstrated exemplary courage and self-sacrifice,” DA Tucker said in his statement.

“His willingness to put his own life at risk to serve and protect the citizens of the Commonwealth likely prevented an even greater tragedy,” Tucker concluded.

Meanwhile, Tribu is the subject of a probe by the Investigation and Enforcement Division of the Massachusetts Alcoholic Beverages Control Commission (ABCC). The ABCC has issued notice to Tribu alleging multiple violations of civil regulations for serving alcoholic drinks after hours and for serving free drinks. A hearing on those allegations is set for next Tuesday (July 28).

Once the ABCC completes its hearing, the Saugus Board of Selectmen – which acts as the town liquor licensing authority – will conduct its own hearing in the case. “The Board is in communication with the ABCC and working collaboratively in this matter,” Board of Selectmen Chair Debra Panetta told The Saugus Advocate this week.

“The ABCC still has an active investigation and will continue to work with us once their investigation is complete. Once the ABCC completes their investigation, the Board can take appropriate action on this matter,” Panetta said. She added that the ABCC requested that selectmen not speak on issues related to Tribu until after the commission’s hearing.

“I am taking my direction from the ABCC,” Panetta said.

“I will call my contact after the hearing for next steps. I have been following this closely, and have been in close communication with the ABCC,” she said.

Board of Selectmen Vice Chair Anthony Cogliano stressed that selectmen need to review the ABCC findings before making a decision. “I think it’s important for all of us to know the facts before we comment on the incident,” Cogliano said.

“I know the ABCC is conducting an investigation and I look forward to hearing what they have to say and if they are offering a recommendation to the Town for us to proceed,” he said.

The investigation into the fatal crash was spearheaded by the Essex County State Police Detective Unit attached to the District Attorney’s Office. The State Police’s Collision Analysis and Reconstruction Section (C.A.R.S.), the State Police Crime Scene Services, the State Police Technical Services Unit, the MassDOT Safety Inspectors, the Office of the Chief Medical Examiner (OCME) and the Lynnfield and Saugus Police Departments also assisted.

“In summary, the investigation revealed the following: Marrero began his evening at a restaurant in Waltham where he purchased food and was provided one complimentary drink; his tab closed out at 7:42 P.M.,” according to the district attorney’s report.

“He then traveled to Tribu Mexican Kitchen and Bar (“Tribu”) on Rte. 1 North in Saugus, approximately a 35-minute drive from the Waltham restaurant. He sat down at the bar at 9:15 P.M. and was served a margarita at 9:20 P.M.; he made a social media posting of a margarita at Tribu with the caption ‘Second stop!!!.’ Between 9:20 P.M. and 12:53 A.M., he was served a total of nine alcoholic drinks at Tribu.”

At 1:56 A.M, Marrero departed the Tribu parking lot in his 2018 Jeep Wrangler and headed north on Rte. 1 in the northbound lane for approximately 2.3 miles. At 2:01 A.M., his vehicle entered the Rte. 1 “jughandle” next to Santarpio’s Pizza in Peabody, which enables drivers to reverse direction onto Rte. 1 South, according to the report.

There are clear, visible, unobstructed and undamaged signs on both sides of the road directing motorists not to enter Route 1 North from the jughandle. MassDOT traffic camera footage shows the Jeep enter the jughandle, graze a guardrail on the right, run a red light, take too sharp a left turn and reenter the northbound lane traveling southbound.

“The Jeep traveled 1.8 miles southbound in the northbound lane. At the Salem Street overpass, the Jeep passed a northbound EZ Disposal truck equipped with dash cam video, which shows the Jeep pass at 2:02:39 A.M.,” the report noted. The truck driver called 911. A Trooper also observed the wrong-way driver and broadcast the information to the State Police Danvers Barracks desk officer and area cruisers. The desk officer responded that he was receiving 911 calls concerning the wrong-way driver.

Trooper Trainor had just completed an assigned public safety detail on the Lynnway in the area of the General Edwards Bridge and was headed home at 1:52 A.M. His cruiser radio was on and set to the channel over which the communications about the wrong-way driver were broadcast.

Trooper Trainor’s cruiser was the first to encounter the Jeep and collided head-on with it. At the point of impact, EDR data showed the cruiser speed was between 60.4 and 68.6 miles per hour and the Jeep between 56.4 and 61.4 miles per hour. Moments after the 911 calls, other responding Troopers came upon the site of the collision, approximately 350 feet south of mile marker 59.2.

“The reconstructionist determined that Marrero’s wrong-way driving was not caused by any defect, malfunction, or condition of the Jeep or any defect or condition of the posted signage,” the report noted. “Rather, ‘the collision was caused by the actions of Mr. Marrero and, furthermore, that his wrong-way operation of the Jeep ultimately resulted in his own death as well as the line of duty death of Trooper Kevin Trainor’.”

Tribu will not face criminal charges, according to the district attorney’s office. “After a thorough review in light of the applicable legal standards and burden of proof, this Office has determined that criminal charges are not sustainable,” the report said.

On May 7, the Alcoholic Beverages Control Commission (ABCC) Investigation and Enforcement Division received information that Marrero may have been at Tribu on May 5 and into May 6, just prior to the crash. At the outset of its investigation, the ABCC learned that the license allowed the sale of alcoholic beverages up to midnight on Monday through Wednesday. Tribu did not seek an extension of alcoholic beverage service hours for Tuesday, May 5, into Wednesday. Documents provided to The Saugus Advocate show that Marrero received five of his nine alcoholic drinks after midnight – the last one was served at 12:53 a.m.

Herman Zambrano, a manager of Tribu, told Supervising Investigator Caroline L. Guarino that Marrero “was his friend and mentor for over 20 years.”

“Zambrano admitted that Marrero did not pay for the Old-fashioned alcoholic beverages or any of the alcoholic beverages served or delivered to him after Midnight,” Guarino wrote in her report.

Several of the drinks after midnight included Macallan 12 Year Whisky.

news_communities.title: Revere Housing Trust Launches Buy Down Program for Homebuyers
news_communities.author: Jim Mitchell
news_communities.summary:
By Barbara Taormina

 

The Affordable Housing Trust Fund Board is gearing up for their next big program to make homeownership possible for low-to-moderate-income buyers. The board’s Buy Down program is a pilot homeownership assistance initiative that will help eligible households close the gap between market prices and what is considered affordable in exchange for deed restrictions and profit sharing with the trust. The Buy Down program reduces the difference between the sales price of a home and an affordable price for low-to-moderate-income buyers. It differs from the down payment assistance program, which is a flat $10,000 grant. The board has acknowledged that the $10,000 down payment has not kept pace with local home sales prices. The Buy Down program is unique because it will lower the purchase price and create genuine affordability.

The board is aiming to create deed-restricted, owner-occupied homes while supporting Revere families who are priced out of the current market. Homes purchased through the Buy Down program will carry a deed restriction for the first 10 years. If the home is resold, it must be resold at an affordable price. From 10 to 20 years, if there is a profit from the sale of the home, the homeowner and Trust split the profit. After 20 years, the homeowner owns the property outright with no affordability restrictions.

The Buy Down program is funded by the Affordable Housing Trust, the Community Development Block Grant and state assistance with a capped amount of help per household.

At their meeting earlier this month, the board discussed advertising the program and designing the applications to be eligible for the program applicants — must have a local connection, such as two years of residency in Revere or have a child in the Revere school system. Applicants must also fall into the low-to-moderate-income range. Applications will be accepted on a rolling basis and will be available in English, Spanish, Portuguese and Arabic.

Board members plan to engage local banks and lenders in Revere, so they understand the program and can work with its requirements. They also intend to blanket the city with flyers, light up social media with information and do whatever it takes to get the word out to residents who might benefit from this opportunity.

Applications for the Buy Down program are expected to be available this fall.

news_communities.title: Former Mass. teacher charged with rape of child
news_communities.summary:
A former teacher in Lynn, Massachusetts, has been arraigned on a charge of child rape involving a 14-year-old middle school student.

Tymond Tran, 38, of Hudson New Hampshire, appeared in Essex County Superior Court on Wednesday where he pleaded not guilty to rape of a child aggravated by an age difference of ten years or more, indecent assault and battery on a person 14 or over, exhibiting a child in a nude or lascivious pose, and distributing obscene matter to a minor, District Attorney Paul F. Tucker announced.

The alleged crimes occurred during the 2022-2023 school year, when Tran was an eighth-grade teacher at the Thurgood Marshall Middle School in Lynn, the Essex County DA’s office said.

Tran worked in Lynn Public Schools from 2021 to 2024 and left the district on his own, without achieving professional status, according to a statement from Lynn Mayor Jared Nicholson and Superintendent of Schools Molly Cohen that was obtained by the Boston Globe.

“We are shocked and disturbed by this news,” the statement read. “Our primary concern is for the well-being of the former LPS student. Our hope for that student is healing and justice.”

The mayor and superintendent also said they are conducting an independent investigation, and will make support available to the school community.

The Globe additionally reported that Tran was named one of Amazon’s Future Engineer Teachers of the Year in 2024 while he still worked at Thurgood Marshall. He won a $5,000 cash prize for the award, and the school was given $25,000.

Tran most recently worked at Lowell Community Charter Public School, the Globe added. He was “immediately terminated” last month after details of his criminal charges — which do not involve any Lowell Community Charter students — were made available, according to the school’s executive director.

“Like all employees, this individual underwent all required state and federal criminal background checks prior to employment,” Nicholas Leonardos said in a statement to the Globe. “Because the charges were not filed until June 2026, those required background checks did not identify any criminal charges or convictions at the time of hire.”

At Tran’s arraignment on Wednesday, the judge ruled that he must wear a GPS and be on house arrest 24/7 except for emergency medical, court, and attorney visits; have no unsupervised contact with anyone under 18; submit to random device searches; reside at an Attleboro address; stay away from and have no contact with any victim or witness; and not possess any child sexual abuse material.

Tran, who was represented in court by attorney Megan Stanley, is due back for a pre-trial conference on Aug. 26.

news_communities.title: La Colaborativa Celebrates Adult Ed and Workforce Development Grads
news_communities.author: Record Staff
news_communities.summary:
Special to the Record

La Colaborativa recently celebrated the graduation of more than 230 students who successfully completed its Adult Education and Workforce Development programs during the Winter and Spring 2026 cohorts.

The ceremony recognized the achievements of community members who are building brighter futures through education, workforce training, and economic opportunity.

A total of 262 certificates were awarded across 10 educational and workforce development programs, including ESOL, Citizenship, Computer Skills, Digital Education, and Carpentry Pre-Apprenticeship.

Each graduate represents a story of resilience, determination, and perseverance. Many balanced work, family responsibilities, and other challenges while pursuing new skills that will open doors to greater economic mobility and long-term success.

One of the evening’s most significant milestones was the recognition of 12 graduates from La Colaborativa’s Carpentry Pre-Apprenticeship Program, all of whom have already been accepted to begin their pathway into the North Atlantic States Regional Council of Carpenters, launching careers in the skilled trades.

The ceremony also featured an inspiring address from Loci Bay, whose journey embodies the transformative impact of La Colaborativa’s programs. Bay first enrolled as a student in the organization’s ESOL and Digital Education classes and now serves as a Teaching Assistant, helping empower the next generation of adult learners.

“Education creates opportunity, strengthens families, and transforms communities,” said La Colaborativa leadership during the celebration. “Every certificate awarded represents determination, resilience, and the belief that a better future is possible.”

La Colaborativa’s Adult Education and Workforce Development initiatives provide immigrant and working families with the knowledge, technical skills, language proficiency, and career pathways needed to achieve economic mobility and build stronger futures.

The organization congratulates the Winter and Spring 2026 graduating class and celebrates the beginning of the next chapter in their educational and professional journeys.

news_communities.title: ZBA Updated on City Zoning and Planning Initiatives
news_communities.author: Record Staff
news_communities.summary:
By Adam Swift

The zoning board of appeals took advantage of a light agenda at its July meeting last week to get an update on planning and zoning issues in the city.

The one special permit and variance that was granted was a relatively minor one, allowing for the construction of a first floor landing and stairway at a nonconforming residence on Central Ave.

Three other hearings, including the proposed residential construction at 320 Revere Beach Parkway, were continued to the ZBA’s next meeting on Tuesday, August 11.

Will Ceccio and Adam Roy of the Permitting and Land Use Planning office provided the planning and zoning updates to the board, something they said they will be doing on a regular basis.

The recently adopted zoning amendments that were touched on included the Accessory Dwelling Unit (ADU) ordinance, the West Chelsea Mixed Use Overlay District, the Fitzpatrick-Prattville Smart Growth Overlay District, and the newly revised inclusionary housing ordinance.

“The accessory dwelling units (ordinance) was adopted in April of this year,” said Roy. “It essentially allows ADUs in all single-family zoning districts. What we did with this ordinance is that the state provided us with minimum guidelines, and we took those guidelines and adopted them to meet the Chelsea context.”

If the ADUs, also known as in-law apartments, meet the requirements in the ordinance in the prescribed districts, they can be built by right.

“ADUs have been permitted that are by right, so they are spurring up in the city,” said Ceccio.

The West Chelsea Mixed Use Overlay District was one of the city’s larger planning initiatives that was spurred by efforts in the West Chelsea area around Market Basket and the commuter rail station over the past several years, according to Roy.

“It is a very large area that was highlighted in our comprehensive planning effort as an area for growth and transformation, especially given its transit accessibility in that area and its lack of residential housing currently,” said Roy. “What we wanted to do in this district is allow for a lot of large-scale, mixed use, transit-oriented development. With that, we wanted to emphasize building form, open space, walkability, and really try to create this area as a new place to go to in Chelsea, allowing these types of developments that would be attractive to bring in Chelsea residents, having that green space and open space in the area, and creates a corridor along Everett Avenue from the transit station to Broadway that is more walkable and pedestrian friendly.”

The Fitzpatrick-Prattville overlay district is similar to the overlay district that led to the Central Avenue Duo project and the reconstruction of Chelsea Housing Authority units there.

The new overlay district will allow for the replacement of the CHA units in the Prattville district, as well as new market rate residential development.

“Paul Nowicki, the director of the Chelsea Housing Authority, (has) talked about how those buildings, those public housing units were built in 1949 and 1951 and essentially haven’t had any significant updates since, so they are in massive need of updating and redevelopment,” said Ceccio.

Roy and Ceccio also touched on the updated inclusionary housing zoning ordinance, which city leaders introduced to help spur development while still maintaining an avenue for developers to build affordable housing units.

Roy and Ceccio also touched on ongoing zoning and planning initiatives, including the Chelsea Pa’Lante master planning process, an affordable housing overlay district, the examination of minimum parking requirements in the city, and green zoning initiatives.

Ultimately, Ceccio said that in the next several years, the city will be looking at a major rewrite of its existing zoning regulations.

“Reforming our zoning is the number one priority,” said Ceccio. “We have a zoning code that does not align with the city’s vision, and it is just extremely outdated and has been updated piecemeal. So we are really taking a step back and looking at this entire zoning code, how can we make something that’s holistic and comprehensive and actually matches the vision that this comprehensive plan is now laying out.”

news_communities.title: Licensing Commission Moves two Disciplinary Hearings to August Meeting
news_communities.author: Record Staff
news_communities.summary:
By Adam Swift

At its regular July meeting last week, the Chelsea Licensing Commission continued two major disciplinary hearings in order to give the establishment owners time to confer with legal representation.

The commission began the disciplinary hearing for King Tacos at 388 Broadway (formerly Las Vegas restaurant).

According to police, near the end of May,  the restaurant served an underage 19-year-old man who then started a fight in the bar and destroyed property. In addition, officers said that video evidence from King Tacos showed on-duty staff drinking in the bar.

The attorney for the restaurant owner, Jeff Turco, stated that he was unable to attend the July hearing and asked that it be continued to the commission’s next meeting on August 13.

Ranchos Las Pupusas at 36 Second St. was also before the commission for alleged violations that took place at the restaurant at the end of May.

According to police, they pulled over a man for operating under the influence in Chelsea in the late afternoon of May 29. Police determined that the man had last been drinking at Las Pupusas before the arrest.

Officers stated that video evidence from the restaurant on that day showed the man drinking eight beers over the course of several hours. In addition, police said the video showed on-duty staff drinking, a violation of the liquor license.

The owner of Las Pupusas stated that he had not received the notice about the July hearing and was not made aware of it until the day of the commission meeting.

The commission members, stating that they had continued the prior hearing under somewhat similar circumstances, also continued the Las Pupusas case until August 13.

Licensing Commission Chair Marnie MacAlpine noted that both continued hearings encompassed serious violations and that the establishments should have their legal representation if they choose, but added that the hearings would move forward at the August meeting without further delays.

In other business, the commission approved a change in the license for Ciao! Pizza and Pasta on Williams Street to allow for alcoholic beverages to be served at outdoor tables. The commission also approved a license application allowing for a DJ at El Potro Mexican Grill at 170 Everett Avenue.

news_communities.title: Chelsea Launches First-Time Homebuyer Down Payment Assistance Program
news_communities.author: Record Staff
news_communities.summary:
Special to the Record

The City of Chelsea is helping make home ownership more attainable by offering a First-Time Homebuyer Down Payment Assistance Program, providing up to $20,000 in financial assistance to income eligible residents purchasing their first home in Chelsea.

The program is run by Chelsea’s Department of Housing & Community Development in partnership with the Chelsea Restoration Corporation, a nonprofit organization that has served Chelsea and surrounding communities for more than 40 years. Through this partnership, eligible first-time homebuyers can receive financial assistance to help cover down payment and closing costs, one of the biggest barriers to purchasing a home.

Eligibility Requirements

• Complete a certified homebuyer education course approved by the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD) such as Chelsea Restoration Corporation’s “First-Time Homebuyer Workshops”;

• Be income eligible based on household size;

• Be a Chelsea resident for at least two years;

• Be a first time homebuyer; and

• Purchase a single-family home or condo located in Chelsea.

Chelsea Restoration Corporation is offering English-language First-Time Homebuyer Workshops on the following dates:

• September 12 & 19, 2026

• October 17 & 24, 2026

Participants must attend workshop sessions to receive the required homebuyer education certificate. Spanish-language workshops took place in July 2026. Additional Spanish-language workshops will take place in 2027.

Residents interested in applying for the Down Payment Assistance Program or registering for the upcoming workshop are encouraged to contact the Chelsea Restoration Corporation at 617-889-2277 or lmalkemus@chelseama.gov.

For more information, visit www.chelsearestoration.org/.

news_communities.title: City Announces New Community Event Series ‘Summer in the Square’
news_communities.author: Record Staff
news_communities.summary:
Special to the Record

The World Cup may have ended, but the City of Chelsea wants to keep its watch-party location, Chelsea Square, as a permanent “third space” gathering spot for its community with “Summer in the Square.”

The City of Chelsea, in partnership with several community-based organizations, announces “Summer in the Square,” a series of free, public programming and community events in Chelsea Square that begins July 21 and will take place every Tuesday, Thursday, and Saturday, throughout the entire month of August, 2026.

“Summer in the Square will build off the success and momentum of our FIFA World Cup Fiesta Fútbol  events series. By continuing to activate this Chelsea Square, we create a welcoming and exciting space where our families, residents, and visitors can connect and play safely. These events will also further support our businesses in the area, many of whom saw increased foot traffic during the World Cup,” said Fidel Maltez, Chelsea City Manager.

Upcoming Summer in the Square Programming:

• Chelsea Farmers Market: Every Tuesday, July 21-October 20, 3–7 p.m.

The Chelsea Farmers Market is making a permanent move to Chelsea Square after previous markets were held outside City Hall, allowing the market to expand with additional vendors and offer a more robust community experience.

• Outdoor Movie Screening of Jaws: Thursday, July 23, at 6 p.m.

Chelsea Recreation & Cultural Affairs will show Jaws on the City’s 9’ x 16’ LED screen, courtesy of Eastern Salt Company.

• Discover Chelsea Car Show & Marketplace: Saturday, July 25, 2–6 p.m.

“Bring your family and friends to experience an exciting day filled with incredible show vehicles, local vendors, live entertainment, raffles, great food, and fun for all ages. Whether you’re a car enthusiast, looking to support local businesses, or simply searching for a great community event, there’s something for everyone!” The Discover Chelsea Car Show will take place in partnership with the Chelsea Chamber of Commerce and the City.

• Outdoor Movie Screening of Pixar’s Elio: Tuesday, July 28, 8 p.m.

Chelsea Recreation & Cultural Affairs will show Pixar Studios’ Elio on its 9’ x 16’ LED screen. In Disney and Pixar’s original, all-new feature film “Elio,” the universe calls back! The cosmic misadventure introduces Elio, a space fanatic with an active imagination and a huge alien obsession. ‘He’s an unusual kid who’s obsessed with getting abducted by aliens,’ says director Domee Shi. Elio feels like there’s nothing for him on Earth—he dreams of going to another world where he’s accepted for who he is.”

• Outdoor Movie Screening of Goat: Thursday, August 6, 8 p.m.

Chelsea Recreation & Cultural Affairs will show the movie Goat on its 9’ x 16’ LED screen. From Sony Pictures Animation, Goat follows Will, a small goat with big dreams who gets a once-in-a-lifetime shot to join the pros and play roarball – a high-intensity, co-ed, full-contact sport dominated by the fastest, fiercest animals in the world. Will’s new teammates aren’t thrilled about having a little goat on their roster, but Will is determined to revolutionize the sport and prove once and for all that “smalls can ball!”

• Outdoor Movie Screening hosted by The Reel Do Over: Thursday, August 13, 6:30-8 p.m.

Devra Zabout of the Reel Do Over, Chelsea’s first and only film festival, will host a night of local documentaries, including Chelsea, The Jewish Years and Chelsea Black Community.

• Inflatable Playzone at the Farmers Market: Tuesday, August 18, 3–7 p.m.

Explore the inflatable playzone during the Chelsea Farmers Market complete with an inflatable moon bounce, obstacle course, and slide.

• Fiesta Fútbol Community Appreciation Celebration: August 18, 4–7 p.m.

Join the Fiesta Futbol team for one last hurrah to celebrate the success of Fiesta Fútbol with the entire community. All are welcome for an afternoon of music, free food while supplies last, and more. 

• Comedy Night: Saturday, August 22, 5:30 – 7:30 p.m.

Join us for a free comedy open mic show in Chelsea Square hosted by Dorothy Whitfield and produced by Devra Sari Zabot, On Clark Ave Productions.

During the event series, the City will keep 2nd Street, from Park Street to Broadway, closed to support the continued community programming and public safety.

Additional Summer in the Square programming will soon be announced. For the ever-growing schedule, and more information, visit www.discoverchelseama.com/summer-in-the-square.

news_communities.title: Star of the Show: Armando Rivera helped Chelsea tell its Story Well at AAC Awards in Denver
news_communities.author: Record Staff
news_communities.summary:
By Cary Shuman

The Chelsea contingent knew right away that its presentation was well-received at the All-America City Awards in Denver.

First came a standing ovation from the contingents across the country inside the ballroom at the Grand Hyatt Hotel. Then came the praise from the judges themselves.

“Guys, that was awesome. It’s clear you practiced and you invested a lot in that, and you did hard work and it paid off,” said one judge.

Added another judge, “Thank you, Chelsea. I get goosebumps seeing the way that you declared The Chelsea Way and the way you’re coming together from many different walks of life.”

Then came the most exciting review of the day from a third judge, “First of all, you all should take this show on the road. I feel like this should be seen on Broadway.”

Armando Rivera, the man who directed the amazing seven-minute performances, could finally smile. If the AAC Awards were the Oscars, Rivera would have taken home the award for Best Director.

With its precise timing, catchy physical elements, and dancing finale – all complemented by a brilliant script composed by Deputy City Manager Devon Fields and the script presentation team – Chelsea was clearly in a class by itself.

“[City Manager] Fidel Maltez had a vision of the Bad Bunny halftime show, so we really wanted to make it a theatrical production, and that’s when we knew we needed to loop in Teatro Chelsea and luckily Armando Rivera was able to join us as a director,” said Fields. “And that really brought us to the next level.”

One of the most well-constructed moments was Police Officer Damian Strait – a gentleman who looks like he stepped right out of a fitness magazine – overseeing pushups by Build The Base students, exemplifying how exercise and teamwork can build discipline. For sheer emotion, the words of Shiara Lara about her personal recovery and The Hub’s contribution to Chelsea were memorable and compelling.

City Manager Fidel Maltez said candidly that he wasn’t sure that the Chelsea group could accomplish everything in the wide-ranging script. “I was skeptical in the beginning because it was a very complicated production,” said Maltez. “But Armando proved me wrong. He had everyone just like it was a finely tuned Swiss watch.”

Rivera humbly took all the praise in stride.

“I’m just glad to have offered all my theatrical talents and skill set to the presentation,” said Rivera.

Rivera has an accomplished background is in the dramatic arts. He is a theater actor and director and the co-artistic director at Teatro Chelsea, where he is a producer as well.

Rivera, 35, is a graduate of Florida Gulf Coast University in Fort Myers. He studied theater and holds degrees in theater and history.

His introduction to theater was bolstered by his home’s proximity to Disney World in Lake Buena Vista. “I went to high school in Kissimmee, Florida. I always like to say that I don’t know how many times I’ve been to Disney, but it’s somewhere between 100 and 200,” said Rivera.

Rivera said after he was given the Chelsea team’s script, he consulted with Maltez and watched previous presentations at the AAC Awards. “What I noticed was that we could do a lot of showy stuff, but the core is to tell the story of Chelsea, and make sure that the people who were being featured, that we got to know them.”

Rivera felt the presentation needed what he described as “our heart moments.”

“You need to have multiple places where you can touch someone’s heart, and thankfully the presentation had heart in abundance,” said Rivera.

The rehearsals in Denver were the spaces where Rivera truly sparkled. His title as director involved telling some of Chelsea’s most consequential community leaders, including Maltez and La Colaborativa President and CEO Gladys Vega, what would work best in the presentation.

“I even had to tell Gladys to just breathe a little bit,” said Rivera. “It’s not about acting better, it’s about slowing down and being able to just emphasize the right parts of your story, and that’s what I hope we accomplished. Obviously, the National Civic League recognized part of that.”

Rivera also made a last-minute audible in Denver, opting to conclude the presentation with a dance number. “For me, it was a beautiful moment and an opportunity to show that culture isn’t just  a food, it isn’t just music, but it’s the way that we move through the world, and to see the image of twelve of our group come out – from youth to police officers, law enforcement, educators, city councillors – they all sat together, they danced together, and they moved in unison, which told a larger message about the actual type of community that exists.”

Inspired by the support and overall leadership of Maltez and Fields and the confidence and trust instilled in him by the entire group’s willingness to listen and take directions, Rivera brought that community together for seven wondrous minutes on a stage in Denver.

And the team representing Chelsea accomplished the goal it had so ardently pursued: a second consecutive All-America City Awards and a spot in the annals of the country’s most prestigious civic competition.

news_communities.title: NeighborHealth Appoints Maria Coppenrath as CFO
news_communities.author: Record Staff
news_communities.summary:
Special to the Independent

NeighborHealth announced the appointment of Maria Coppenrath, CPA, as the organization’s new Vice President and Chief Financial Officer, effective July 20, 2026.

With more than 30 years of healthcare finance experience – including over 15 years serving as CFO within complex, multi-site Federally Qualified Health Centers (FQHCs) – Coppenrath brings deep expertise in healthcare finance strategy, value-based care, 340B pharmacy operations, ACO models, and FQHC regulatory compliance.

Most recently, Coppenrath served as CFO at Caring Health Center in Springfield, Massachusetts. She has also held senior financial leadership roles at Greater Lawrence Family Health Center and Edward M. Kennedy Community Health Center, where she led financial transformation initiatives, strengthened organizational sustainability, and supported significant operational growth.

Coppenrath succeeds Jamie Hazard in the CFO role following Hazard’s transition to Chief Executive Officer in December of 2025. In her role as CFO, Coppenrath will oversee financial strategy, planning and operations for one of the largest community health centers in the country, with more than $300M in annual revenue, 1700 employees, and a full complement of primary care and specialty services, a 24/7 emergency department, the Neighborhood PACE program, and multiple on-site pharmacies.

“Maria stood out throughout the search process for both her exceptional financial leadership and her deep commitment to community health,” said Jamie Hazard, President and CEO of NeighborHealth. “She brings a rare combination of strategic expertise, operational excellence, and mission alignment. We are thrilled to welcome her to NeighborHealth and are confident she will be an invaluable partner as we continue to strengthen and expand care for the communities we serve.”

Born and raised in Everett, Massachusetts, Coppenrath distinguishes herself through her collaborative leadership style, ability to translate complex financial concepts into actionable insights, and proven track record of building high-performing finance teams.

“NeighborHealth is well known for its innovation, compassion, and service, and I am honored to be part of an organization so deeply committed to equitable, community-based care,” said Coppenrath. “As a first-generation Italian American and the daughter of immigrants, I understand the hopes, sacrifices, and resilience that shape so many of the families NeighborHealth serves. That personal connection makes it especially meaningful to join an organization that has long been a trusted resource for immigrant communities. I look forward to partnering with the leadership team and staff to support NeighborHealth’s continued growth, strengthen its mission, and ensure its long-term sustainability.”

Coppenrath joins NeighborHealth following a highly competitive national search. She is the recipient of numerous professional recognitions, including the National Association of Community Health Centers’ Jeffrey T. Latman Leadership in Healthcare Finance Award, and serves as Treasurer on the Board of Directors of the Massachusetts League of Community Health Centers. She is also on the Board of Directors of CapitalLink, which provides financial advisory services to community health centers in Massachusetts.

NeighborHealth, formerly East Boston Neighborhood Health Center, is one of the nation’s largest Federally Qualified Health Centers (FQHCs) and the largest community-based primary care health system in Massachusetts, serving more than 120,000 people and recognized by the Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) as a National Quality Leader. For more than 50 years, NeighborHealth has offered access to comprehensive primary care, specialty care and emergency care for patients in the Commonwealth. In a first-of-its-kind merger in 2020, East Boston Neighborhood Health Center joined forces with the South End Community Health Center and expanded services to patients who live and work in Boston’s South End and Roxbury neighborhoods. NeighborHealth champions innovative care models to actively bridge health equity gaps in our low-income and immigrant communities with services unique from other healthcare providers in our state. For more information, visit www.neighborhealth.com.

news_communities.title: Developer withdraws Western Ave plans
news_communities.author: Olivia HaydarOlivia HaydarOlivia Haydar
news_communities.summary:
LYNN — Singh Realty LLC will withdraw its proposal to build a six-unit multifamily housing development at 30-32B Western Ave. following concerns raised by neighborhood residents.

Residents opposed the project over concerns about increased traffic on Western Avenue, both to and from Route 107, parking for existing residents and future tenants, pedestrian safety, the impact of construction vehicles on the roadway, and whether the proposed building would fit the neighborhood’s character of coastal- and Cape Cod-style single-family homes.

At a neighborhood meeting on July 16 hosted by Ward 2 City Councilor Obed A. Matul, Matul said he planned to vote against the special permit petition at the Aug. 4 City Council meeting in response to residents’ concerns. Following the meeting, Matul contacted Singh Realty attorney AJ Capano to discuss the neighborhood’s objections to the proposal.

Capano said his clients did not want to create an adversarial relationship with the neighborhood and decided to withdraw the proposal.

“After further consideration of the comments and concerns raised during the public review process, we’ve determined that additional time is appropriate to reevaluate the proposed design and consider potential revisions to the project,” Capano said. “So, on Aug. 4, we are going to respectfully request the council approve the withdrawal without prejudice.”

Capano said withdrawing the special permit application will allow the development team, including its architect and engineer, to revise the project to better address neighbors’ concerns.

Matul said he will continue to advocate for residents throughout the development process.

“As a ward councilor, I always have to look out for what is best for my ward and my community. I have heard the concerns of my constituents and will stand beside them. I wish the developers at 30 Western Ave. the best and hope they come back with a new proposal that will fit in the community and neighborhood,” he said.

Matul added, “We do need housing; however, it must be something that fits in perfectly with the neighborhood and community. We are looking forward to making this property something that fits properly with our neighborhood. I want to thank every concerned constituent who came to our neighborhood meeting to make their voice heard.”

Capano said he does not yet know what revised plans his clients may pursue, but said they will not move forward with the proposed six-unit multifamily building.

He said he will submit a formal letter to the Lynn city clerk before Aug. 4 requesting permission to withdraw the special permit application, as required under Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 40A and the city’s zoning ordinance.

The post Developer withdraws Western Ave plans appeared first on Itemlive.

news_communities.title: Marblehead continues search for a chief financial officer
news_communities.author: Olivia McKenna-StevensOlivia McKenna-StevensOlivia McKenna-Stevens
news_communities.summary:
MARBLEHEAD — The Select Board discussed the ongoing search for a new chief financial officer and a new survey system called FlashVote that will bridge a communication gap at its meeting Wednesday.

Town Administrator Thatcher W. Kezer III gave an update on the current search for a chief financial officer to replace Aleesha Benjamin, who will depart from her position in August. 

Due to a fast turnaround, the board is still searching for an applicant to fill this position. Kezer suggested that, to ensure the role is not left unfilled for too long, a temporary measure could be taken to appoint a skilled treasurer from across committees or outsource through the consultant agency Clifton Larson Allen. 

This temporary appointee would not be given the exact salary an official chief financial officer would make; however, they would be compensated. These measures would be temporary, and the board will have further discussions on what the best next step will be.

Jeff Sereni and FlashVote CEO Kevin Lyons introduced a new survey service that will allow the town of Marblehead to better understand what their constituents need. In addition, the public will have the ability to share their opinions through an anonymous forum. 

FlashVote creates the panels and surveys based on Marblehead’s demographics and the issues most pressing to constituents. This program helps to bridge the gap between constituents and city government to make communication feel more streamlined, even if it is anonymous. 

Like any town, Marblehead is not without its grievances toward the town government. The hope is that this service will create a baseline for more open and honest communication on the issues that are most pressing for Marblehead. 

In an exciting turn of events, the Planning Board is welcoming a new member, Susan Koelle.

Koelle owns and operates her own architecture business, Susan Koelle Architect LLC, where she and a small team serve the greater Boston area. 

Koelle has been embedded within North Shore communities for a long time and plans to use her time on the planning board to continue the betterment of Marblehead. 

At Wednesday night’s meeting, Koelle was selected as the newest member of the Planning Board, where she will bring her expertise and a love for the Marblehead community. She will be sworn in Thursday morning. 

The post Marblehead continues search for a chief financial officer appeared first on Itemlive.

news_communities.title: Lynn organizations unite against ICE
news_communities.author: Dylan PichnarcikDylan PichnarcikDylan Pichnarcik
news_communities.summary:
LYNN — The clouds seemed to part at just the right moment Wednesday evening, as the rain stopped, the community gathered on the steps of Lynn District Court to hold a vigil for those impacted by ICE

The event was sponsored by the North Shore Labor Council of Massachusetts, with hosts of Lynn’s LUCE Immigrant Justice Network of Massachusetts Hub and Nueva Primavera.

Dozens gathered with signs in hand and on display with photos of individuals who are victims of ICE attacks. With Candles lit, the community spoke out against ICE.

Local Pastor Eduardo Cáceres led a prayer to begin the vigil.

“We hope this prayer reaches you and brings comfort to those; we hope that every tear that is shed brings comfort; we ask you to open heaven to those people who lost their lives; we hope that this brings comfort to all of those who have lost family in these trying times,” he said.

Members of the Lynn community also spoke at the vigil, including Silvia, whose husband was arrested by ICE agents on his way to work on July 1, 2025. Her children, one of whom also spoke and highlighted that he missed his father greatly.

“I’m so thankful that we’ve been able to come here to this city where at first we felt really welcome and really safe, but now because of everything that’s going on we no longer feel that,” she said, her words being translated by LUCE Volunteer Yaira Matos.

She said, “I thank God that I can be here on this ground, and that we can all be gathered here today to look at all of these people who have been removed.”

Another woman, Judith, said her husband was detained by ICE while at Lynn District Court. The officers broke his arm. Now, due to the attack, it no longer functions as it should. She was also translated by Matos.

Judith said she has not seen her husband since June 14, and he missed his child’s birthday.

She said her child needs their father around and “we need that extra support, and right now we’re trying everything we can to get him out, but it doesn’t look like it’s going to happen any time soon, and it’s been really difficult for us because we’re calm people, we’re very humble people.”

Eni, another speaker who was translated by Matos, said, “We need to lift our voice and say no to ICE and their kidnapping of people, because realistically they’re taking way too many people, including my husband.

“There have been so many deaths because of immigration, so many victims, so many families are left without their fathers and their mothers; we have to fight,” she said.

news_communities.title: Clams and students jam in Nahant
news_communities.author: Olivia HaydarOlivia HaydarOlivia Haydar
news_communities.summary:
NAHANT — More than 20 students trekked down to Doggie Beach on Wednesday morning to survey local species as part of the Coastal Ocean Science Academy Program. 

The program, hosted by Northeastern University’s Marine Science Center in Nahant, was established in 2007 to connect students of all ages to local coastal ecology, which has kick-started an early love for nature in many. 

By using nature as a classroom, students entering sixth, seventh, and eighth grade from Nahant, Swampscott, Marblehead, Lynn, Revere, Boston, Melrose, and even New York, receive a week-long hands-on experience in what it is like to be a scientist in the Littorina program, said Sierra Muñoz, the Outreach Program coordinator and lecturer for Northeastern’s Department of Marine and Environmental Sciences. 

At Doggie Beach, the 21 middle schoolers broke into groups of five to eight, cycling through three survey stations. The first was an epifauna and infauna survey where they identified different bivalves like clams and mussels on the surface of the sand (epifauna) and beneath the surface of the sand (infauna). This was done at the three different tidal exposures: the high tide zone, the intertidal zone, and the low tide shoreline zone to decipher differences in biodiversity. 

The second survey focused on testing how much light can pass through the water (water turbidity); wind decibels; how salty the water is; the water temperature; and hypothesizing how it all impacts the health of the environment. 

For the third survey, students dressed in chest-high waders, went a couple of feet into the ocean, and threw out a seine net, which is a large fishing net that lies on the surface of the water. Here, students researched what species thrived off the shore. What they found were tiny silverside fish and a variety of shrimp. 

One of the most lasting lessons from the program, however, has been understanding how to make the environment better than how the students left it, Muñoz added. 

“These beaches and rocky shores are classrooms, and the concepts teach themselves,” Muñoz said. 

The program also offers an experience for students entering ninth through twelfth grade called the Chondrus program, where the participants go more in depth about weather conditions, water quality, specific species biodiversity, and relating them to potential local threats along the marshy, sandy, and rocky locations around the North Shore. 

As a professor of the science communication course at Northeastern for undergraduates in the College of Science, the most rewarding element has been working with undergraduate students who then become trained in leading lessons for middle and high schoolers in the programs. 

Incoming senior Northeastern Undergraduate Emily Stangel has a double major in environmental sciences and journalism, with a minor in marine biology. 

“I absolutely love science communication, and you know every kid says they want to be a marine biologist when they’re little, and I just never grew out of that phase,” Stangel said. Writing about the science she has had a lifelong passion for, she gets to “convey complex science ecosystems and biodiversity,” making it digestible for everyone to understand so they know how to play a part in conserving the Earth, she added. 

One of the younger leaders of the program, Mia Bianchini, is a soon-to-be Swampscott High School ninth grader. She shared that her favorite part of being in the program for two years has been seeing the curiosity for science and nature as a whole inspire other youth. 

“One of the main things for me is just being able to see the love for the ocean from other people, and I think that’s really important, especially with the state of the environment now,” Bianchini said. “Getting the newer generation to appreciate nature is really, really important.” 

Bianchini expanded on her love for the environment and how she hopes for it to open the minds of others: “I think that everyone should at least try to take some course, some experience that teaches them about nature because you really get a deeper appreciation of what goes on.”

news_communities.title: Former Lynn teacher arraigned on child rape charges
news_communities.author: Sophia HarrisSophia HarrisSophia Harris
news_communities.summary:
LYNN — A former Lynn Public Schools teacher who was nationally recognized during his time in the classroom pleaded not guilty Wednesday in Essex County Superior Court to multiple charges alleging he sexually abused a 14-year-old student during the 2022-23 school year while teaching at Thurgood Marshall Middle School.

Tymond Tran, 38, of Hudson, New Hampshire, was arraigned before Judge John C. Fraser on charges of rape of a child aggravated by an age difference of 10 years or more, indecent assault and battery on a person age 14 or older, exhibiting a child in a nude or lascivious pose, and distributing obscene matter to a minor.

According to the Essex County District Attorney’s Office, the alleged offenses involved a then-14-year-old student while Tran was working as an eighth-grade teacher at the Lynn middle school. Tran pleaded not guilty to all charges.

Assistant District Attorney Kate MacDougall represented the Commonwealth. Tran was represented by attorney Megan Stanley. A pretrial conference is scheduled for Aug. 26.

Judge Fraser ordered Tran released under strict conditions pending trial. He must wear a GPS monitor and remain on house arrest except for emergency medical care, court appearances and attorney visits. He also is prohibited from having unsupervised contact with anyone younger than 18, must submit to random electronic device searches, reside at an Attleboro address, have no contact with any alleged victim or witnesses, and may not possess child sexual abuse material.

Mayor Jared C. Nicholson and Lynn Public Schools Superintendent Dr. Molly Cohen issued a joint statement following the arraignment.

“We are shocked and disturbed by this news,” Nicholson and Cohen said. “Our primary concern is for the well-being of the former LPS student. Our hope for that student is healing and justice. We also recognize that this news will be deeply upsetting for students, families, staff, and the broader school community, and we will be making support available as needed. We will continue to fully cooperate with law enforcement and are also conducting our own independent investigation.”

According to Lynn Public Schools, Tran worked in the district from 2021 to 2024 and left voluntarily before attaining professional status.

Tran received national recognition during his tenure in Lynn. In 2022, he was named one of 10 recipients nationwide of Amazon’s Future Engineer Teacher of the Year Award, an honor recognizing educators who expand access to computer science education in underserved communities.

As part of the award, Tran received a $5,000 cash prize, while Thurgood Marshall Middle School was awarded $25,000 to upgrade aging computer equipment. At the time, he was credited with launching the school’s first computer science program and developing lessons designed to meet students with varying interests, skill levels and learning needs.

The Essex County District Attorney’s Office emphasized that all defendants are presumed innocent unless and until proven guilty in a court of law.

The post Former Lynn teacher arraigned on child rape charges appeared first on Itemlive.
