news_communities.summary:
[This story first appeared on Boston Restaurant Talk.]

A North Shore ice cream spot that has been around for a long time has shut down.

According to multiple sources, Dairy Witch in Salem will not be reopening for the season, with a Facebook post from the Boston Street spot saying the following:

After 74 years, Dairy Witch is closing. That is not an easy sentence to type. There is a weight to it. Bea and Pete Polemenako opened the window at 117 Boston Street in 1952, the same year their daughter Marietta was born. Marietta has stood by that window her entire life, but will be retiring this year. We will not be opening this spring. There will not be another season….We’ll be sharing more in the coming days. Old photos, stories, and a few things we’ve been working on, so a piece of Dairy Witch can stick around even after the window stays closed. For now we just wanted Salem to hear it from us before anyone else. Thank you.

Dairy Witch has been known over the years for its variety of ice cream flavors along with its sundaes, banana splits, frappes, freezes, malts, and more.

(Follow Marc on Bluesky at @marchurboston.bsky.social)

[A related post from our sister site (Boston’s Hidden Restaurants): List of Restaurant Closings and Openings in the Boston Area]

news_communities.title: Road rage fight caught on camera in Lynn
news_communities.author: Oscar Margain and Matt Fortin
news_communities.summary:
A man was hospitalized after a road rage incident Friday morning in Lowell, Massachusetts.

The incident escalated into an assault at a car wash on Route 1A. It’s being investigated by Massachusetts State Police, who responded shortly before 9:30 a.m..

Video from the scene showed traffic at a standstill as several people got out of their cars and started fighting in the roadway.

Two men were seen throwing punches while others tried to break it up.

A third man can be seen knocking someone to the ground.

“People have an ego,” said Jay Lopez, a customer at the car wash. “When somebody doesn’t back down, they have like, ‘Oh, I’m not going back down either.’ And then they just have to go back and forth until somebody goes to sleep.”

He feared it could have gone worse.

“It could have been a gun,” he said. “All you’ve got to do is mind your business. Drink water, mind your business.”

State police said the man who was knocked down suffered a laceration. He was taken to an area hospital.

No arrests have been made.

The case remains under investigation.

news_communities.title: 3 charged with human trafficking at Salem spas
news_communities.summary:
Police in Salem, Massachusetts, said three people are facing charges after a monthslong human trafficking investigation into several businesses.

Authorities said they investigated suspected trafficking and prostitution-related activities at businesses advertising “bodywork,” “reflexology” and “spa” services over the last three months.

Detectives executed search warrants Friday at businesses including New Custom Body Work and Red Rose Reflexology, both on Boston Street, police said Wednesday. They did not say whether more businesses were searched.

Salem police said 57-year-old Peter Leavitt of Peabody, 52-year-old Fang Liu of Peabody and 64-year-old Barry St. Onge of Shrewsbury were taken into custody and will be charged with trafficking of a person for sexual servitude, deriving support from prostitution and keeping a house of prostitution.

The Salem Police Department noted that human trafficking “involves the recruitment, harboring, transportation, provision, or obtaining of individuals for the purposes of forced labor or commercial exploitation.” It said in a press release that it “emphasizes that individuals involved in these situations are regarded as victims and are treated accordingly throughout the investigative process.”

Authorities did not say when the suspects would be arraigned or if they had attorneys.

“We see this as much a rescue operation as it is a criminal investigation,” Salem Police Chief Lucas Miller said in a statement. “The exploitation of victims of human trafficking will not be tolerated in the city of Salem.”

news_communities.title: East Boston getting popular gourmet doughnut/sandwich shop
news_communities.author: Boston Restaurant Talk
news_communities.summary:
[This story first appeared on Boston Restaurant Talk.]

The team behind a handful of gourmet doughnut shops and sandwich shops in the Boston area will be bringing the two concepts to a waterfront location.

According to a Facebook reel from Gallows Group founder Rebecca Roth Gullo, Blackbird Doughnuts and Sally’s Sandwiches will be opening in East Boston, moving into a space at Clippership Wharf. In the reel, Rebecca mentions that there will also be a “little sprinkling of The Gallows,” which was a gastropub that resided in Boston’s South End until closing in 2021, and that the business plans to open sometime in the late spring or early summer.

Locations of Blackbird Doughnuts can be found in Boston’s South End, Beacon Hill, Fenway, Brighton, Cambridge’s Harvard Square, Newton Centre, and Watertown, while Sally’s Sandwiches can be found in the South End, Harvard Square, and Brighton. The website for all locations can be found at blackbirddoughnuts.com.

(Follow Marc on Bluesky at @marchurboston.bsky.social)

news_communities.title: Fiery crash into Saugus gas pump caught on camera
news_communities.summary:
A gas pump burst into flames after being hit by a vehicle Wednesday in Saugus, Massachusetts.

The crash was caught on camera. The vehicle can be seen pulling off of Route 1, slamming into the pump at a Speedway station.

Massachusetts State Police said they responded to the crash shortly after 2 p.m.

No injuries were reported, police said.

The vehicle was towed from the gas station.

Police did not say whether any charges would be brought.

news_communities.title: Chelsea High School teacher on leave after allegations of inappropriate behavior
news_communities.author: Griffin Gonzalez
news_communities.summary:
A high school teacher in Chelsea, Massachusetts, has been placed on leave after allegedly having inappropriate conversations with students.

Parents and students say the inappropriate behavior went on for years. The district took action after students staged a walkout at Chelsea High School this week.

“For the past three years, I’ve been enduring a teacher who has been inappropriate towards myself and other students,” 17-year-old Jelannie Hunter told the Chelsea School Committee this week.

“When you’re working with children, there are boundaries,” her mother told NBC10 Boston in an interview Thursday.

She said she went to the school for the past three years complaining of the teacher’s conversations with her daughter.

Students at Chelsea High School walked out of class Wednesday to protest the lack of action taken by the district despite complaints from students and parents.

“As of today, the employee involved has been placed on leave,” Chelsea Public Schools said in a letter to the Chelsea High School community Wednesday. “The school district has also initiated an independent investigation of the concerns raised regarding this matter.”

The district also said in the letter that while it “encourages and supports students’ constitutional rights to freedom of speech and expression,” it planned to discipline some students who stayed in the halls longer and “and disrupted the instructional day for themselves and others who did not participate.”

NBC10 Boston is not naming the teacher, as no charges have been filed against her.

We reached out to the district to see whether or not she would be paid throughout her leave and why action was taken now after years of complaint, but have not received a response.

news_communities.title: As Artemis II crew splashes down, Navy sailor from Mass. was set to greet them
news_communities.author: Thea DiGiammerino
news_communities.summary:
A U.S. Navy sailor from Massachusetts was expected to be among the first people to greet the Artemis II astronauts when they returned to Earth Friday evening.

Chief Hospital Corpsman Vlad Link is a member of the dive medical team that was set to open the Orion capsule, the U.S. Navy said in a media release. Link, who is from Chelsea, was expected to help get the astronauts out of the capsule safely after splashdown and make an initial medical assessment of their condition.

Link has 18 years of experience in dive medicine.

“I have been exposed to the Navy since I was a young teenager, and I’m proud to represent both my family and hometown,” Link said in the media release. “Contributing our efforts to NASA and the Artemis II mission is something we take great pride in as part of that legacy.”

NASA astronauts Reid Wiseman, Christina Koch and Victor Glover and Canadian astronaut Jeremy Hansen returned to Earth on Friday evening after 10 days in space. This involved a fiery plunge through the atmosphere, ending in a splashdown in the Pacific Ocean off the coast of San Diego.

news_communities.title: Teen held without bail on murder charge in Chelsea stabbing
news_communities.author: Mike Pescaro, Marc Fortier, Griffin Gonzalez and Sebastian Agudelo Gomez
news_communities.summary:
A teenager has been charged with murder in connection with a deadly stabbing on Thursday night in Chelsea, Massachusetts.

Sergio Josué Castellanos, 18, was held without bail at his arraignment Friday. He was allowed to hide his face in court.

Police responded to the intersection of Chestnut Street and Everett Avenue around 6:45 p.m. Thursday, and said multiple people were injured in stabbings.

Authorities said Friday that two people were injured: Castellanos, who suffered minor injuries, and 25-year-old Eduardo Rózales López of Chelsea.

Prosecutors with the Suffolk County District Attorney’s Office said López was treated at the scene for stab wounds to his neck and torso. He was transported to Massachusetts General Hospital, where he died from his injuries early Friday morning.

Reviewing video, police described seeing five people chasing after López. A man identified as Castellanos could be seen striking López in a stabbing motion while he’s on the ground, prosecutors said.

The video allegedly showed Castellanos and three other people fleeing. Police found him and three other males behind the fence of a home nearby on Chestnut Street.

Castellanos, who was tending to a stab wound to his leg, had changed his clothes from those seen on camera, which were found next to him, prosecutors said. He was transported to an area hospital for treatment.

Authorities said a black machete with stains on the blade was recovered.

Castellanos’ defense attorney, asking that his client be released on bail with GPS monitoring, raised questions about the identity of the attacker. He noted that in the video in which prosecutors described Castellanos making a stabbing motion, no knife was seen.

The judge ordered Castellanos held without bail.

Family members told Telemundo Nueva Inglaterra that López arrived from Guatemala seven years ago and leaves behind a 2-year-old child.

People in the area were shocked by the confrontation.

“It was kind of scary, I’m not going to lie. You still see the remnants of blood on the floor,” neighbor Shauniece White said Thursday. “It’s extremely concerning. As you see, it’s only been one nice day so far. If it’s already happening now, I can only imagine what the summer has in store for us. I’m hopeful that the police will come out and do due diligence to keep the community safe, but wishful thinking in a time like this.”

Massachusetts State Police are assisting Chelsea police with the investigation.

news_communities.title: Multiple people hospitalized after being stabbed in Chelsea
news_communities.author: Griffin Gonzalez and Mike Pescaro
news_communities.summary:
Police in Chelsea, Massachusetts, said multiple people were injured in stabbings Thursday evening.

Officers responded to the intersection of Chestnut Street and Everett Avenue around 6:45 p.m.

While the Chelsea Police Department did not say how many people were stabbed, it confirmed all those hurt were hospitalized, including one person who is in serious condition with potentially life-threatening injuries.

Authorities did not say whether anyone had been arrested.

“It was kind of scary, I’m not going to lie. You still see the remnants of blood on the floor,” said neighbor Shauniece White. “It’s extremely concerning. As you see, it’s only been one nice day so far. If it’s already happening now, I can only imagine what the summer has in store for us. I’m hopeful that the police will come out and do due diligence to keep the community safe, but wishful thinking in a time like this.”

Massachusetts State Police are investigating alongside the city’s department.

“Chelsea Detectives and Mass State Police Detectives are currently working this very serious incident and we are ready to utilize any and all of our resources to give our community the peace of mind our citizens deserve,” Chelsea Police Chief Keith Houghton said in a statement.

Anyone with information is asked to call 617-466-4800. Anonymous tips can also be left by calling 617-466-4880.

news_communities.title: Danvers murder suspect was spotted wandering around Lynn before his arrest
news_communities.author: Amelia Fabiano
news_communities.summary:
A young man raised alarm on Thursday as he wandered streets and backyards in Lynn, Massachusetts, carrying a knife. Neighbors who saw him were shocked to learn that he’s accused in a murder in Danvers.

Multiple people told NBC10 Boston that they saw 18-year-old Anthony DeMayo walking around at about noon Thursday with a large knife in his hand. Later, he would be identified as the suspect in the murder of Janet Swallow in Danvers.

Several of them called 911, which led police to arrest him in a backyard on Standish Road. The whole thing was caught on home surveillance video.

“They said they saw a crazy guy with a knife, and I’m like, ‘How did this happen,’ you know? It’s scary, you never think, where you live, it’s going to happen,” John Bossi said.

Security cameras captured the moments leading up to the arrest. It starts with a vehicle crashing into Nells Pond in Lynn around 11:40 a.m.

“It’s hard to even get there. I don’t know how he got through. There’s no easy way for a car to get there,” Bossi said.

About 13 minutes later, DeMayo is seen walking down Bow Ridge Road in a bright green shirt, swinging a knife around in his right hand.

Then, he goes to Nells Pond Road. Ashley O’Brien was outside with her ducks when she saw him walk by.

“I thought for sure that this guy was obviously mentally unwell. I didn’t want to make a scene and run away or anything like that, cause he made eye contact with me,” she said.

She immediately called 911, the first of several calls from the neighborhood.

“I just waited till he passed my house, went in, told my husband and called the cops,” O’Brien said. “When we came back out, he was just kind of stabbing all the bushes outside and running a knife across the houses and stuff like that — it was very bizarre.”

Rhonda Deparolesa was another on nearby Standish Road.

“I was sitting on my chair, in the recliner, and all of the sudden, I see somebody jump over my fence,” Deparolesa said. “He just laid flat on his back on the bulkhead and just wasn’t moving, so my husband was upstairs, and I said, ‘Something’s going on here.'”

Deparolesa said he tried to open her door at one point, but thankfully, it was locked.

“What if he did get in my house? My husband and I might not be here,” she said. “You know, you think you’re safe in your house. Sometimes you’re not.”

By around 12:30 p.m., police were after him. They found him in a backyard a few doors down from Deparolesa’s home. He was arrested and placed in a police cruiser.

“I’m just absolutely shocked that a young kid, 18 years old, had his whole life ahead of him, and had to go and do this,” Deparolesa said. “For what? Why?”

Everyone who spoke to NBC10 Boston was shocked to learn DeMayo is accused of murder.

“I just thought this guy needed some help and I just called. I did not think that he’d just murdered somebody,” O’Brien said.

Police confirmed DeMayo was a student at Bishop Fenwick High School in Peabody. They did not comment on the investigation, but said they are fully cooperating with local law enforcement.

Their thoughts and prayers are on the victim, her family, and everyone impacted by the horrific tragedy. They said their focus right now is on staff and students.

They had additional counselors on campus Friday and will on Monday, as well.

news_communities.title: New details revealed in court about Malden teacher accused of raping high school student
news_communities.author: Kaitlin McKinley Becker
news_communities.summary:
A 54-year-old teacher accused of raping a high school student in Malden, Massachusetts, faced a judge Thursday, a day after his arrest.

Authorities allege Scott Marino gave alcohol to an 18-year-old who is known to him and then sexually assaulted her last Wednesday, Feb. 18, at his Stoneham home, where the victim was currently living. Marino was arrested a week after the alleged incident and has been charged with two counts of rape, one count of indecent assault and battery and one count of furnishing alcohol to a person under the age of 21, according to the Middlesex County District Attorney’s Office.

Prosecutors provided shocking new details Thursday in Woburn District Court at the arraignment for Marino, who teaches at Linden STEAM Academy in Malden and coaches the girls’ basketball team at Malden High School.

Resources for victims of sexual assault are available through the National Sexual Violence Resources Center and the National Sexual Assault Telephone Hotline at 800-656-4673, and Massachusetts provides this list of statewide and resources for sexual assault survivors.

Jacob McCrindle of the Middlesex County District Attorney’s Office said in court that the alleged victim in this case had been in the foster care of Marino and his wife at the time this occurred. The victim, who moved in with the Marinos at their Stoneham home in the fall, was a basketball player for Marino during the season, and the two were known to each other for many years.

According to prosecutors, Marino’s wife was scheduled to take a trip out of state last Wednesday. The victim was on school vacation, so she went with Marino and a grandfather who lives in a basement apartment at the home to an Ash Wednesday mass. They dropped the older man off at home, and then Marino and the victim went driving because she had a learner’s permit and was learning to drive on the highway. They made the decision to go into Boston to get a takeout seafood dinner, and on the way home, Marino had them stop at a liquor store where surveillance video shows he purchased a strawberry margarita mix, a large bottle of Patron tequila and some other agave liquor.

The two then went back to Marino’s Stoneham home and he allegedly gave her a large margarita. She drank some of it but didn’t like it, though Marino allegedly encouraged her to keep drinking. He later poured them shots of tequila, and they ate the seafood dinner they got. The victim later told police that she was feeling ill so she went to her bedroom where she vomited many times, eventually lying down in bed.

Marino then came in and sexually assaulted her two times, prosecutors said in court. The victim later retrieved her cell phone and began texting friends and ultimately an aunt, begging for help.

McCrindle said that the aunt called 911 as she was driving to the Marino’s home, and the victim was taken to Winchester Hospital for a full sexual assault examination.

“This is a relationship where the defendant was the victim’s foster parent, he took her into his home, that’s a position of trust, as well as coupled with the fact that the defendant is a teacher, or was a teacher at the Malden Public Schools and he was also her coach for many years,” McCrindle said in court, arguing for $100,000 cash bail. “The defendant used I would suggest that relationship, he took advantage of that relationship, there’s a significant age difference, the defendant is 54 years old, the victim is 18 years old. There’s also a significant size difference that’s described in the police report between the defendant and the alleged victim…. it’s also noteworthy that the defendant appears to have waited until his wife left the home so he was more isolated with the victim…clearly some planning went into this.”

Marino’s defense attorney argued before the judge that he’s a respected educator with deep community support who has “never been in court in his life.”

The judge ultimately ordered $75,000 cash bail based on the gravity of circumstances along with several conditions requested by the commonwealth, including that Marino stay away and have no contact from the victim and any witnesses, stay away from all Malden school property and events, be fitted with a GPS bracelet to enforce exclusive zones including the victim’s home address, her school, her workplace and a gym that she frequents, surrender his passport and not be allowed to leave the state.

The judge also added the condition that Marino be alcohol free and submit to SCRAM testing.

Resources for victims of sexual assault are available through the National Sexual Violence Resources Center and the National Sexual Assault Telephone Hotline at 800-656-4673, and Massachusetts provides this list of statewide and resources for sexual assault survivors.

Malden Superintendent Timothy Sippel previously said the district was informed Wednesday that Marino had been taken into custody in connection with “a serious incident alleged to have occurred outside of working hours and involving one of our high school students.”

Marino was immediately placed on leave, the superintendent added, noting that the district is cooperating fully with law enforcement agencies in their investigation.

“I understand how upsetting and distressing this is to our community given the serious nature of these allegations,” Sippel said in a statement. “The safety of our students and staff is our highest priority.”

A plea of not guilty was entered on Marino’s behalf. He is due back in court for a probable cause hearing on April 16.

news_communities.title: Malden teacher and coach charged with rape of teen
news_communities.author: Mike Pescaro
news_communities.summary:
A teacher and coach in Malden, Massachusetts, has charged with the rape of an 18-year-old.

Malden Public Schools said Scott Marino had been arrested over a “serious incident” involving a high school student. The Middlesex County District Attorney’s Office and Stoneham Police Department later said he had been charged with two counts of rape, one count of indecent assault and battery and one count of furnishing alcohol to a person under 21.

Marino is accused of providing alcohol to the victim, who is known to him and sexually assaulting her on Feb. 18, prosecutors said.

Superintendent Timothy Sippel said Marino teaches at Linden STEAM Academy — which serves kindergarten through eighth grade — and is the head coach of the Malden High School girls’ basketball team.

Sippel said that Marino’s “arrest is in connection with a serious incident alleged to have occurred outside of working hours and involving one of our high school students.”

“I understand how upsetting and distressing this is to our community given the serious nature of these allegations,” the superintendent said.

Marino is due to be arraigned Thursday in Woburn District Court. It was not immediately clear if he had an attorney.

news_communities.title: Girl rescued after falling through the ice in Malden
news_communities.author: Thea DiGiammerino and Mike Pescaro
news_communities.summary:
Firefighters rescued a teen girl who had fallen through the ice into the frigid water of a pond in Malden, Massachusetts, on Wednesday afternoon.

It happened around 2 p.m. at Fellsmere Pond, fire officials said. Firefighters in water rescue suits went in to find the girl holding onto the edge of the ice, according to the Malden Fire Department. She was pulled from the water in under 30 seconds.

“I don’t even think the engine stopped,” Malden Fire Captain Roy Tribble said. “I jumped up, I sprinted down here, pretty much just jumped right into the water.”

The 14-year-old girl was conscious and alert when she was brought to a Boston hospital for treatment.

Tribble said the water was about 30 feet deep.

“A life was saved. It was a young girl who fell through the ice,” added Captain Bob Hegarty.

He warned everyone to stay off thin ice.

“A warning to everybody out there that ice, if it’s not thick enough, you’re going to fall through, and if you fall through, you’re not going to be able to get yourself out,” Hegarty said. “So stay off the ice until it’s very thick, which — who can tell when it’s very thick?”

news_communities.title: Tree falls on 2 homes in Malden

There was no immediate word on any injuries.

Pictures from the scene showed the tree split in half, falling into the front yards.

news_communities.title: Malden woman accused of threatening federal agents making immigration arrest
news_communities.author: Thea DiGiammerino
news_communities.summary:
A Massachusetts woman is accused of threatening to kill federal agents who were conducting immigration enforcement in Malden.

Bethany Abigail Terrill, 37, was charged with threatening a United States official, the U.S. Attorney’s Office said Thursday.

Prosecutors say that Terrill approached the agents as they were making an arrest outside Malden District Court on Sept. 29, describing her as verbally abusive. She allegedly shouted things like “ICE is here, ICE is here,” “You guys are monsters, this is insane,” “Sir, what’s your name, what’s your name,” “I can try to help you,” and “I am an American civilian, I have a right to be here,” and called the agents Nazis.

She is further accused of trying to push past the agents, yelling “Charlie Kirk died, and we love it… We’re coming for you, gonna kill you.” 

According to the statement of probable cause, an agent heard the threat and proceeded to detain Terrill, who they said was “actively resistant and fought to escape.”

Officials say the agents were wearing body cameras, and Terrill was also filming at the time. In the statement of probable cause, investigators say the threat can be heard in the footage from Terrill’s phone.

The agents on scene were from multiple agencies, including Homeland Security Investigations (HSI), ICE and the FBI.

During her appearance in U.S. District Court in Boston on Thursday, a public defender said Terrill owns a salon and was struggling with legal issues in an unrelated case.

Judge Paul Levenson ordered Terrill be released with conditions, saying “federal agents have a right to not be subjected to threats.”

Terrill was ordered as part of her conditions of release to participate in a mental health program for treatment on a regular basis, to develop a safety plan for her own safety and others, avoid contact with potential witnesses, have no contact with any federal official, family or otherwise in any way or form, refrain from alcohol and the use of any illegal drugs, to avoid contact with law enforcement, and to refrain from making any further threats.

She didn’t speak to reporters outside of court.

The charge of threatening a U.S. official carries a sentence of up to 10 years in prison, three years of supervised release and a fine of $250,000.

news_communities.title: Cities bordering Everett unite in Revolution soccer stadium transparency push
news_communities.author: Asher Klein
news_communities.summary:
Four cities that border Everett, Massachusetts, have banded together in a push for more involvement in the New England Revolution’s plans for a soccer-specific stadium in the city.

The mayors of Revere, Medford, Malden and Chelsea formed a coalition called City Mayors for Citizens that they said will seek transparency on plans for the stadium, including the impacts it will have on their communities.

The 25,000-seat stadium proposal has progressed in the state Legislature but still faces plenty of hurdles. The City Mayors for Citizens said they are worried about how the building on along the Mystic River might impact local infrastructure, public transit and quality of life.

“A project of this size and scale will have significant regional impacts,” said Revere Mayor Patrick Keefe Jr said in a statement. “We want to ensure that a stadium is contributing positively to the communities it is drawing resources from. Our residents should not bear the burdens of a private stadium that has strong potential to negatively affect their day-to-day lives.”

Asked for a response to the statement, a Revolution club spokesperson noted that it expects to work with neighboring communities after it reaches agreements with Everett and Boston, which would allow the property to have its port designation removed and the process of construction to continue.

The spokesperson also noted that the team thanked the mayors and promised to review the concerns raised in their letter, sent Monday.

news_communities.title: Man shot in Saugus road rage incident
news_communities.summary:
A man was shot and injured Wednesday evening in a road rage incident in Saugus, Massachusetts, according to police.

Officers responded shortly after 8 p.m. to the reported shooting on Lincoln Avenue in Cliftondale Square. The incident happened outside Banana Splitz, an ice cream shop.

The victim, a 32-year-old man from Melrose, was found shot in the leg, police said. He suffered a serious but non-life-threatening leg injury.

Police arrested Stephen Cooper, a 48-year-old Saugus man, who remained at the scene of the shooting.

Authorities did not specify charges against Cooper, a licensed firearm carrier, but noted that he is facing several gun charges.

“His license to carry is pending suspension by the licensing authority,” a Saugus police spokesperson said in a statement.

No further details were immediately available.

news_communities.title: Video shows Salvadoran man being detained by ICE in Malden
news_communities.author: Oscar Margain, Marc Fortier and Mark Garfinkel
news_communities.summary:
The wife of a Salvadoran man says he was violently taken into custody by immigration officials on his way to work in Malden, Massachusetts, on Wednesday morning.

The incident occurred shortly before 6 a.m. in the area of 28 Kennedy Drive.

Leslie Perlera Gonzalez said she received a distressing call from her husband, Edgar Hernan Elias Escobar, shortly before 6 a.m. as he was heading to his construction job. She rushed to Kennedy Drive, where she said Elias Escobar was surrounded by immigration officers.

“I heard them smash his window, and he was saying, ‘Ow, you’re hurting me,'” Perlera Gonzalez said. “When I arrived on the scene, he had a Taser on his face.”

The 31-year-old had no criminal record and was in the process of adjusting his immigration status after the couple married last year, noted Perlera Gonzalez.

“He’s never come close to doing anything that would be considered a crime,” she said.

She alleged that agents used an unmarked car to ram her husband’s vehicle, forcing him off the road before detaining him.

Malden police said Elias Escobar sustained minor injuries. He was transported on a stretcher to a hospital in Melrose, where Perlera Gonzalez said she was not allowed to see him.

About an hour later, he was moved to a U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement processing facility in Burlington.

A spokesperson from the Department of Homeland Security said in a statement that Elias Escobar was the target of the ICE operation and suffered “no injuries.”

“On September 17, 2025, ICE conducted an immigration enforcement operation in Malden, Massachusetts to arrest Edgar Hernan Elias-Escobar, an illegal alien from El Salvador. During the arrest, the illegal alien refused to comply with officer commands to rolldown his window. The officers took appropriate action and followed their training to use the minimum amount of force necessary to resolve the situation in a manner that ensured the success of the operation and prioritized the safety of the public and our officers,” the spokesperson said in a statement. “Out of an abundance of caution, ICE law enforcement escorted Elias-Escobar to the hospital for evaluations. Medical staff found he had no injuries and discharged him. He was then escorted to the Boston Filed office for processing and will be placed in removal proceedings.”

“I’ve been expecting this from somebody I know every day since Donald Trump was elected,” said Amiee Parco, an attorney with Greater Boston Legal Services and a colleague of Perlera Gonzalez. “I’ve been waiting for it to affect me personally.”

Friends and coworkers gathered outside the Burlington facility, joining a weekly protest to support Elias Escobar’s release and that of other detainees.

“He’s a really wonderful person,” said Alyssa Rao, a friend of the couple. “I think this is going to impact everyone.”

After Wednesday’s ordeal, Perlera Gonzalez fears what her husband is going through while detained.

“I’m terrified of what could be happening when no one is watching,” she said.

At last check, Escobar was still at the facility. It’s unclear how long he’ll be held — often, people stay several days, though the facility is meant to detain people for hours.

His lawyer is trying to prevent the government from sending him to a far-away detention center, and from deportation.

ICE has ramped up arrests across greater Boston in recent weeks under “Operation Patriot 2.0,” detaining immigrants in public spaces.

Gov. Maura Healey spoke out against the sweeps on Tuesday, saying federal immigration enforcement officers are “creating an incredible amount of fear and intimidation.”

“Some of it seems purposeful, to be honest. Why else are you parked outside a church? Why are you parked outside a community center?”

The governor said her office has received no information from ICE about who is being detained or why.

“This is not a furtherance of public safety,” she said. “In fact, it undermines public safety.”

Asked what she can do to protest White House policy, Healey said: “I join with other governors in urging the Trump administration, in the strongest possible terms, to change course.”

State House News Service contributed to this report.

news_boston.title: Starbucks in South Station cleared to reopen after rodent infestation
news_boston.author: Marc Fortier and Malcolm Johnson
news_boston.summary:
A Starbucks in Boston’s South Station has been cleared to reopen after dealing with a rodent infestation.

The Starbucks was shut down by the city last week after Boston Inspectional Services said it found rodent droppings throughout the store, along with “multiple violations of the Massachusetts Sanitary Code and the FDA Food Code.” Video shot by NBC10 Boston also captured a rodent scampering around inside the shuttered store.

The city suspended the store’s food permit and ordered the business to stop food operations immediately, saying it would have to deep clean and sanitize the store, hire a pest control company, dispose of any contaminated food and correct all health code violations before it would be allowed to reopen.

The Inspectional Services Department said it conducted a follow up inspection on Tuesday of this week and found three dead mice on a glue trap, leading them to continue the temporary suspension until appropriate mitigation measures could be verified.

In an email Thursday, the Inspectional Services Department said another follow up inspection verified that all sanitary code violations had been corrected.

“As a result, Starbucks has been authorized to reopen and resume normal business operations,” they said.

news_boston.title: Steve Kerrigan, chairman of Mass. Democratic Party, arrested for OUI
news_boston.author: Marc Fortier
news_boston.summary:
Steve Kerrigan, the chairman of the Massachusetts Democratic Party, was arrested for drunken driving last month in Southborough.

Court records show that Kerrigan, 54, of Lancaster, was charged with operating under the influence of liquor, negligent operation of a motor vehicle and a marked lanes violation on June 13. He pleaded no contest at a hearing in Westborough District Court on June 15, and the case was continued without a finding for a year. He also lost his driver’s license for 45 days.

NBC10 Boston reached out to Kerrigan for comment on the arrest. He released the following statement:

“In June I was pulled over for a marked lane violation and blew a .089 on the breathalyzer. I regret and take full responsibility for my actions and their consequences and appreciate the Southborough PD and Westborough Court for their professionalism and service.”

Kerrigan was arrested around midnight on June 13 on Route 9 in Southborough after his 2017 BMW X3 was seen driving in the middle of both lanes, directly on top of the dotted white line, according to police.

Police said Kerrigan told them he was going home from a restaurant. They said his eyes appeared glassy and bloodshot red. There was no odor of alcohol, the officer wrote in his report, “due to an overwhelming odor of cologne.” His speech appeared “slow, thick tongued, and slurred,” the officer noted.

The officer said he asked how much alcohol Kerrigan had, and he said he had two beers around 6 p.m. before going out to dinner.

Kerrigan took a breathalyzer test, which registered a blood alcohol content of .089%, over the legal limit of .08%.

news_boston.title: Dog, puppies found in unsafe conditions; NH woman charged with animal cruelty
news_boston.author: Marc Fortier
news_boston.summary:
A New Hampshire woman is facing animal cruelty charges after a dog and seven newborn puppies were found living in unsafe conditions.

Back on May 31, Hudson police said they responded to a local residence following dog barking complaints. During their investigation, officers learned that a female Cane Corso had recently given birth inside a detached garage. The mother and seven puppies were found living among multiple potential hazards.

The garage contained trash and debris, exposed electrical wires, batteries, deteriorating drywall and an open container of gasoline near the dogs’ food and water bowls and the puppies were located on several soiled blankets, police said.

Based on the condition of the garage and concerns for the safety and welfare of the animals, animal control determined that the mother and puppies should be removed from the property.

A follow-up investigation determined that the mother dog had originally given birth to 10 puppies, three of which had not survived.

The legal owner of the dogs was not present at the residence at the time of their removal but was later identified as 50-year-old Candice Riley, of Hudson. Riley was arrested Wednesday at her home on a warrant charging her with eight counts of cruelty to animals. She was released on personal recognizance bail and is scheduled to appear in Nashua District Court on Tuesday.

The mother and puppies were signed over to the Animal Rescue League of New Hampshire. They said two of the puppies have already been adopted, and a few more are pending.

news_boston.title: Training camp preview: Expectations for Maye, the latest on Jacas, and more
news_boston.author: Justin Leger
news_boston.summary:
Rejoice, New England Patriots fans. Training camp is almost here.

The Pats will return to the practice fields behind Gillette Stadium for Day 1 of camp on Saturday at 10:30 a.m. ET. As they prepare to get back to work, our insiders Tom E. Curran and Phil Perry are back in the podcast studio to get you up to date on the biggest Patriots storylines heading into training camp.

It all starts with third-year quarterback Drake Maye, who will look to build off his NFL MVP runner-up campaign with even better numbers now that he has a true No. 1 receiver in A.J. Brown. Then, there are some not-so-fun storylines, such as second-round linebacker Gabe Jacas remaining unsigned and star cornerback Christian Gonzalez still looking for a lucrative contract extension.

Curran and Perry cover it all in the latest Patriots Talk Podcast:

MORE PATRIOTS

New England Patriots Jul 17

A.J. Brown: Patriots QB Drake Maye a ‘true leader'

The next step for Drake Maye

Of course, the center of attention at Patriots training camp will be Maye. After leading New England to a Super Bowl appearance and finishing second in MVP voting, Maye could be even more dangerous this year with more weapons at his disposal.

The Patriots traded for superstar wide receiver A.J. Brown and signed ex-Green Bay Packers wideout Romeo Doubs to strengthen their passing game. At camp, it’ll be worth watching how Maye builds chemistry with his new receivers while taking on an even larger leadership role with a crowded WR room.

“You’re gonna have, with Drake Maye, an onus on him to be the lead person,” Curran said. “(Offensive coordinator Josh) McDaniels can do it, wide receivers coach Todd Downing can do it, Mike Vrabel can do it. But really, it falls to the guy in the huddle to assuage concerns among A.J. Brown or Romeo Doubs or Kayshon Boutte or DeMario Douglas about their touches, because there’s gonna be a lot of guys who are saying, ‘Where’s mine? What’s mine? What’s my role?’ because there are new roles being established.

“I think the onus is on him in Year 3 of his career to begin to have that leadership that (Tom) Brady took on with his wideouts to get in their ear and say, ‘Relax, you’ll get plenty of balls.'”

Perry believes Maye’s focus will be on the satisfaction of his new No. 1 wideout.

“I think the one guy he has to concern himself with in that regard would be A.J. Brown,” Perry said. “I think having that experience that they had last year with Stefon Diggs will help (Maye) a lot. I think his confidence, just the comfort that he has in his own skin will allow him to continue to deal with guys who are producing at a superstar level.

“If Kyle Williams starts barking at him or Kayshon Boutte if he’s here starts chirping at him in the locker room … like, sorry guys, you’re not at that level. And Drake Maye’s at a level now where he’s runner-up in the MVP where he can kind of look at them and say, ‘Are you serious right now?'”

The Gabe Jacas dilemma

Patriots second-round draft pick Gabe Jacas still hasn’t signed his contract with training camp looming. Albert Breer of Sports Illustrated said on Early Edition that the young linebacker is “inching toward a redshirt rookie season.”

Who’s to blame for this situation?

“I think I’m going to begin with Jacas,” Curran answered. “It seems as if the agitation is with a surgery based upon some Mike Reiss reporting that he had in the offseason on his shoulder. There was a knee issue prior to that, and he also had a shoulder injury that was surgically repaired.

“What Mike reported was that the hangup was the Patriots had something in a waiver for him to participate in the offseason prior to signing his full contract that he didn’t agree to. So I’m feeling like the first shot was fired by a surgery that seemingly, the Patriots may not have had knowledge of. But resolution has to happen with a guy who was supposed to, I’d imagine, contribute this year.”

Perry is left perplexed by how the ordeal has unfolded with no apparent solution in sight.

“I wonder how long this is gonna go, because Gabe Jacas is posting on social media all sorts of clips of him moving around at a pretty good rate,” Perry said. “And so, is he physically unable to do anything right now? Is he 90 percent not 100 percent, and he’s holding himself back? I don’t know. … He’s still the only draft pick who’s unsigned. … It makes it a very unusual situation for all of us covering it.”

Also in the episode: A Christian Gonzalez contract update, the best day to attend training camp, and more.
